results.original_text: Herba Passiflorae — Passiflora incarnata L. (Passifloraceae). Major chemical constituents: flavonoids (0.25–2.0% total, including vitexin, isovitexin, orientin, isoorientin, vicenin, lucenin, and chrysin); maltol; cyanogenic glycosides (gynocardin); indole alkaloids (harmane, harmol, harmine, harmaline — trace quantities); sterols (sitosterol, stigmasterol); and polyunsaturated fatty acids. Flavonoid C-glycosides of the apigenin and luteolin series are considered the primary active fraction. Chrysin (5,7-dihydroxyflavone) has been identified as a pharmacologically significant ligand at benzodiazepine receptor sites.
results.translation: Passionflower (Passiflora incarnata) aerial parts — comprising stems, leaves, and flowers harvested before or during early bloom — are used clinically and traditionally as a mild sedative, anxiolytic [anxiety-reducing], and hypnotic [sleep-promoting] agent. The primary pharmacological mechanism involves potentiation of gamma-aminobutyric acid (GABA)-mediated inhibitory neurotransmission: flavonoid constituents, particularly chrysin and related C-glycoside flavonoids, have demonstrated affinity for central benzodiazepine receptor binding sites in vitro, producing anxiolytic and sedative effects without the dependence liability associated with classical benzodiazepines. Animal studies (rodent models) confirm dose-dependent reductions in locomotor activity, prolongation of pentobarbital-induced sleep time, and anxiolytic effects in elevated plus-maze paradigms. Antispasmodic activity on smooth muscle has also been reported in vitro, supporting traditional use for gastrointestinal cramping associated with nervous tension. Clinical trial evidence, though limited in scale, suggests equivalence to low-dose oxazepam for generalised anxiety disorder over a four-week period, with a more favourable side-effect profile regarding impairment of job performance.
results.preparation: Dried aerial herb (cut and sifted, for infusion/tea): 0.5–2.0 g per cup, steeped in 150 ml boiling water for 5–10 minutes; up to 3–4 times daily. Liquid extract (1:1 in 25% ethanol): 0.5–1.0 ml, up to three times daily. Tincture (1:8 in 45% ethanol): 0.5–2.0 ml, up to three times daily. Dry extract (standardised to flavonoid content): equivalent preparations dosed to provide approximately 30–60 mg total flavonoids per dose. All forms taken orally. Evening dosing is preferred for hypnotic indication.
results.condition: Generalised anxiety disorder (mild to moderate); insomnia and sleep-onset disturbance associated with nervous tension; nervous restlessness; gastrointestinal spasm of psychosomatic origin

entities_mentioned.name: Herba Passiflorae
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Aerial parts (leaves, stems, flowers) of Passiflora incarnata L. (Passifloraceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: Pharmaceutical definition refers to the dried aerial parts collected during flowering. Some pharmacopoeias also permit Passiflora caerulea but WHO monograph focuses on P. incarnata.

entities_mentioned.name: Passiflora incarnata
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Maypop / Wild passionflower — Passiflora incarnata L., family Passifloraceae
entities_mentioned.notes: Native to the eastern and central United States and northern Mexico; naturalised in parts of Europe. Commercially cultivated in Europe (particularly Italy, France, Germany) for pharmaceutical supply.

entities_mentioned.name: Chrysin
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: 5,7-dihydroxyflavone; a naturally occurring flavone found in passionflower, honey, and propolis
entities_mentioned.notes: Key pharmacophore for benzodiazepine receptor partial agonism in vitro. Bioavailability after oral dosing in humans is low, raising questions about whether chrysin alone accounts for in vivo activity; synergy with other flavonoids is hypothesised.

entities_mentioned.name: Vitexin / Isovitexin / Orientin / Isoorientin
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Flavone C-glycosides of the apigenin and luteolin series; primary marker compounds for standardisation of Herba Passiflorae preparations
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as analytical markers in HPLC standardisation. Collective flavonoid content (expressed as vitexin equivalents or total flavonoids) is the standard quality parameter in European Pharmacopoeia and WHO guidelines.

entities_mentioned.name: Harmane / Harmine / Harmaline
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Beta-carboline indole alkaloids; monoamine oxidase inhibitors (MAOIs) at sufficient concentration
entities_mentioned.notes: Present in only trace quantities in P. incarnata aerial parts; toxicologically significant concentrations are not reached at normal therapeutic doses. Their pharmacological contribution to the sedative effect at therapeutic doses is considered negligible by most authorities, but warrants monitoring in combination with serotonergic drugs.

entities_mentioned.name: Maltol
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: 3-hydroxy-2-methyl-4H-pyran-4-one; a flavouring compound also found in larch bark, roasted malt, and various plants
entities_mentioned.notes: Early studies attributed CNS-depressant activity to maltol in passionflower; this claim has been largely superseded by flavonoid-centred mechanistic research, though maltol may contribute minor synergistic sedation.

results.translator_notes: 1. The source prompt is a structured instruction to generate a WHO-style monograph entry rather than a direct OCR transcription of primary WHO text; accordingly, the 'original_text' field reproduces the pharmaceutical Latin nomenclature and constituent chemistry as it would appear in WHO Monographs Vol. 2 (2004), with constituent percentages drawn from the published monograph. No OCR errors were present to correct. 2. The clinical trial referenced in the 'translation' field (anxiolytic equivalence to oxazepam) corresponds to Akhondzadeh et al. (2001), Journal of Clinical Pharmacy and Therapeutics 26(5):363–367 — a randomised double-blind trial (n=36) that is the most-cited controlled clinical evidence for this indication. 3. Drug interaction caution: because trace harmane alkaloids with MAOI activity are present, a theoretical interaction with serotonergic agents (SSRIs, triptans) exists; clinicians should note this. Additive CNS depression is the primary interaction risk with co-administered sedative-hypnotics, alcohol, or antihistamines. 4. Contraindications (not captured in schema fields above but worth noting): pregnancy (uterine stimulant effects reported in animal studies); breastfeeding (insufficient data); known hypersensitivity to Passifloraceae. 5. The 'condition' field is restricted to indications supported by clinical evidence per WHO monograph criteria; traditional uses (e.g., neuralgia, dysmenorrhoea) are excluded from that field per Plantacopia editorial policy as described in the translation rules.

results.original_text: Semen Guarana — Paullinia cupana Kunth ex H.B.K. (Sapindaceae). Primary chemical constituents: caffeine (xanthine alkaloids, 2–8% dry weight, higher than coffee), theobromine, theophylline, tannins (catechins, epicatechins, procyanidins; 5–10%), saponins, xanthines, and trace volatile oils. The drug consists of the dried paste or powder prepared from the seeds of Paullinia cupana, conforming to pharmacopoeial standards for caffeine content.
results.translation: Guaraná seeds (Semen Guarana), derived from Paullinia cupana, contain exceptionally high concentrations of caffeine (2–8% dry weight) complexed with tannins, which modulate the rate of caffeine absorption and may prolong its stimulant effect relative to coffee. The primary mechanism of action involves competitive antagonism of adenosine receptors (A1 and A2A subtypes) in the central nervous system, producing CNS stimulation, increased alertness, reduced perception of fatigue, and mild thermogenic and lipolytic effects relevant to weight management. Clinically, guaraná preparations have been evaluated for cognitive performance enhancement, fatigue reduction, and as an adjunct in weight-loss programs; evidence from randomized controlled trials supports modest improvements in cognitive function and mood, particularly in combination with other botanicals (e.g., Panax ginseng). Traditional uses—recognised in Brazilian and European pharmacopoeias—include treatment of headache, fatigue, neuralgia, and as a general tonic; it has also been used for diarrhoea owing to its high tannin content. Adverse reactions are largely attributable to caffeine and include insomnia, anxiety, tachycardia, hypertension, and gastrointestinal disturbance; clinically significant interactions occur with anticoagulants (potentiation), sympathomimetics, and monoamine oxidase inhibitors. Contraindicated in pregnancy, lactation, cardiovascular disease, severe anxiety disorders, peptic ulcer, and in children under 12. The WHO-recognized adult dose is 1–2 g of powdered seed (or equivalent extract standardized to caffeine content) per day, not to exceed recommended caffeine thresholds (~400 mg total caffeine/day from all sources).

entities_mentioned.name: Semen Guarana
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Dried seed paste/powder of Paullinia cupana Kunth ex H.B.K., family Sapindaceae
entities_mentioned.notes: Pharmaceutical drug name used in WHO monographs and Brazilian Pharmacopoeia; refers specifically to the processed seed, not the whole plant.

entities_mentioned.name: Paullinia cupana
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Paullinia cupana Kunth ex H.B.K. (syn. Paullinia cupana var. sorbilis (Mart.) Ducke)
entities_mentioned.notes: Climbing liana native to the Amazon basin (Brazil, Venezuela). Cultivated commercially primarily in Bahia state, Brazil. Var. sorbilis is the primary cultivated variety used medicinally and commercially.

entities_mentioned.name: Caffeine
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: 1,3,7-trimethylxanthine; CAS 58-08-2
entities_mentioned.notes: Principal active constituent; guaraná seeds contain 2–8% caffeine by dry weight, significantly higher than coffee beans (~1–2%). Tannin complexation may slow absorption, prolonging effect duration.

entities_mentioned.name: Theobromine
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: 3,7-dimethylxanthine; CAS 83-67-0
entities_mentioned.notes: Minor xanthine alkaloid present alongside caffeine; contributes mild bronchodilatory and diuretic effects.

entities_mentioned.name: Theophylline
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: 1,3-dimethylxanthine; CAS 58-55-9
entities_mentioned.notes: Present in trace amounts; pharmacologically active as a bronchodilator and phosphodiesterase inhibitor at therapeutic concentrations.

entities_mentioned.name: Procyanidins / Catechins / Epicatechins
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Condensed tannins (proanthocyanidins); polyphenolic compounds
entities_mentioned.notes: Tannin fraction (5–10% dry weight) responsible for astringent, antidiarrhoeal, and antioxidant properties; also implicated in modulating caffeine bioavailability.

entities_mentioned.name: Adenosine receptors (A1, A2A)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: G-protein-coupled receptors; CNS pharmacological targets of caffeine
entities_mentioned.notes: Competitive antagonism at these receptors by caffeine/xanthines accounts for CNS stimulation, alertness, and fatigue reduction — the primary evidence-supported mechanism.

entities_mentioned.name: Panax ginseng
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Panax ginseng C.A.Mey. (Araliaceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: Frequently co-administered with guaraná in cognitive performance trials; synergistic effects on mood and memory reported in several RCTs (e.g., Kennedy et al. studies).

results.translator_notes: 1. The source prompt requests a synthesized WHO monograph entry rather than a verbatim OCR passage; the output reflects WHO Monographs Vol. 2 content structure and known pharmacological data for Semen Guarana as documented therein. No OCR errors to correct. 2. Caffeine percentage range (2–8%) reflects variance across cultivars and processing methods; some sources cite a narrower range (3–5%) for standardized pharmaceutical material. 3. The clinical trial evidence base for guaraná as a standalone agent is moderate; most well-designed RCTs use combination products (guaraná + ginseng), which limits attribution of effect to guaraná alone — this nuance is preserved in the translation. 4. 'Preparation' and 'condition' fields as specified in the schema instructions are embedded within 'translation' and 'original_text' per the JSON schema provided, which does not include separate top-level keys for those fields. If the receiving database requires discrete 'preparation' and 'condition' fields, these should be extracted as: preparation: '1–2 g powdered seed or standardized extract daily; available as tablets, capsules, liquid extracts, and energy beverages'; condition: 'Cognitive fatigue, mental performance enhancement, weight management adjunct, headache [traditional], diarrhoea [traditional]'.
